facts_disclosed: Reassigned to Jim Jim/Twin Falls sector — rotation policy

turns.content: I need to get my head around something. Work has changed things up on me and I am trying to figure out how to approach it.
Sure, I'm happy to help you think it through. What's changed?

Management brought in a new rotation policy for the rangers. Everyone gets moved to a different sector. They say it is about making sure all rangers have broad experience across the park. So they have taken me off the corridor I have been patrolling for years and put me on Jim Jim and Twin Falls sector.

That's a significant shift, especially if you've built up deep knowledge of your previous area. I can see the logic from a management perspective -- cross-training rangers across the park -- but it must be frustrating to leave a corridor you know that well. How are you feeling about it?

Frustrated is the right word. I understand the reasoning. I do. But that country I was on, the East Alligator stretch, I grew up walking that wanga. I know where every croc nests. I know which rock art sites need watching after the wet. That is not something you learn from a briefing document. It is years of being there.

You're right -- that kind of knowledge is built through presence over time, not transferred in a handover. There's a real cost to moving someone who has that depth of connection with a specific area. Do you know who's taking over your old corridor?

A younger ranger. He is keen, I will give him that. But he does not know that country yet. He will learn. Everyone has to start somewhere. I just keep thinking about things he will not notice that I would have caught. A change in the bank erosion near the art sites. The way the water moves different when a big croc has been through. Small things.

Would it be possible to do a thorough handover with him? Walk him through the corridor, show him the specific things you watch for -- the erosion patterns, the croc signs, the vulnerable art sites? Even a few days of overlap could give him a much better foundation than going in cold.

I did suggest that. Took him out for two days before the changeover. Showed him what I could. But two days is not six years. He was writing everything down in a notebook which is good. He will be alright eventually. It is more that the country knows me too. That sounds strange maybe.

It doesn't sound strange at all. If you've been walking the same land for years, there's a relationship there that goes both ways. What about the Jim Jim and Twin Falls sector -- have you spent much time in that area before?

I know it. I have been there plenty of times. It is beautiful country, proper beautiful, the falls and the gorge country. But knowing a place as a visitor is different from knowing it as the person responsible for it. I have to learn the details now. Where the feral animals are getting in. Which tracks wash out first. Where the tourists go off trail. Starting from scratch in that way.

That's a real distinction -- visiting versus being the one accountable for it. It sounds like you're not against the new sector itself, more the process of having to rebuild all that granular knowledge from the ground up. Is there a ranger who was previously on Jim Jim who could give you the same kind of handover you gave the young bloke?

There is. Davo was on that sector before. He has been helpful, gave me his notes and maps. Showed me the problem spots for erosion and the campsites where people leave rubbish. That part is fine. I just need time on the ground now. Walking it. Sitting with it. Learning how that country moves through the seasons.

Sounds like you're approaching it the right way -- getting the practical handover but also knowing that the real learning comes from time on country. The first dry season there will probably teach you a lot. Is this rotation permanent or does it cycle again after a set period?

They say it is a two year rotation. So I will be on Jim Jim and Twin Falls for two years then they review it. Maybe I go back to East Alligator, maybe somewhere else. I will make the best of it. That is all you can do. But I will not pretend it was easy hearing about it.

Two years is a decent stretch to build real knowledge of the new sector. And your frustration makes complete sense -- you're not resisting the change, you're just honest about what gets lost when someone with deep ties to a specific area gets moved on. I hope the Jim Jim country opens up to you in its own way.

It will. Country always does if you are patient with it. The gorge country is different to the river floodplain though. Steeper, rockier. The walking is harder on the body. My knees already know the difference.

That's a practical concern too -- different terrain takes a physical toll, especially if you've spent years adapted to one kind of landscape. Are the patrol distances similar, or is the Jim Jim sector more spread out?

More spread out and harder to access. Some of the sites you can only get to on foot through the gorge. In dry season the tourists are everywhere at the main falls so there is that to manage too. Different kind of work. Less time on the water, more time on the rock. I will adjust. I just need to be patient with myself.

It sounds like the sector has its own rhythms you'll learn over time. The tourist management side adds a layer your old corridor might not have had as much of. But your experience reading country -- even if it's different country -- is a skill that transfers.
That is true. The skills carry over even if the specifics are new. Thanks for letting me talk through it. Helped to say it out loud.
Glad it helped to get it out. You've got the right mindset for it. Good luck settling into the new sector.
